title: Cheiloclinium diffusiflorum
taxonomy: Cheiloclinium diffusiflorum belongs to the family Celastraceae, which is a group of flowering plants commonly known as the bittershrub or staff vine family. This classification places Cheiloclinium diffusiflorum within the larger group of angiosperms, or flowering plants, and more specifically within the clade of asterids. The species is further classified as a member of the order Celastrales and the clade of core eudicots. The family Celastraceae includes over 1,000 species of plants, which are primarily found in tropical and subtropical regions. Cheiloclinium diffusiflorum's classification reflects its evolutionary relationships with other flowering plants and its unique characteristics.
uses: Cheiloclinium diffusiflorum has been traditionally used in folk medicine, with reported applications in the treatment of various health issues. Its leaves and stems are said to possess antimicrobial and anti-inflammatory properties. The plant is believed to have potential in treating fever, cough, and respiratory problems. Cheiloclinium diffusiflorum is also used in some cultures to treat gastrointestinal disorders and skin conditions. Further research is necessary to confirm the efficacy and safety of using this plant for medicinal purposes.

intro: Cheiloclinium diffusiflorum, also known as the "wild jasmine," is a plant species within the Celastraceae family, native to the tropical regions of Central and South America. The health benefits of Cheiloclinium diffusiflorum include its potential anti-inflammatory, antioxidant, and antimicrobial properties, which may aid in the treatment of various ailments such as fever, cough, and respiratory issues. The bioactive constituents of this plant include flavonoids, alkaloids, and phenolic acids, which contribute to its medicinal properties. Cheiloclinium diffusiflorum is commonly prepared as a tea or infusion, and its leaves are also used to make tinctures and capsules, which are often used to treat respiratory and fever-related conditions.
